name: SkinMarks
short_description: Rub-on temporary-tattoo skin electronics that turn precise body landmarks into touch, squeeze, and bend input surfaces with integrated visual output.
long_description: SkinMarks are ultra-thin conformal skin-electronic devices, printed with the conductive polymer PEDOT:PSS and applied to the body like temporary rub-on tattoos, that align with fine body landmarks such as knuckles, tendons, and skin folds. They sense touch, squeeze, and bend gestures and provide co-located visual output (e.g. electroluminescent or LED displays), letting people control devices through eyes-free, body-relative gestures. As physical AI and interaction design move computation onto and into the body, SkinMarks demonstrates how the skin itself can become a high-resolution, tangible on-body interface that exploits existing anatomical reference points.
ai_note: A skin-conformal sensing and actuation technique using printed PEDOT:PSS electrodes wired to an Arduino; no machine learning or AI runs in the system.

images.caption: SkinMarks conformal skin-electronic tattoos applied to body landmarks for touch, squeeze, and bend input.
images.alt_text: Thin electronic tattoos printed on a person's fingers and hand, with conductive traces aligned along the knuckles and tendons acting as touch-sensitive buttons.

sources.title: SkinMarks: Enabling Interactions on Body Landmarks Using Conformal Skin Electronics
SkinMarks: Enabling Interactions on Body Landmarks (project page)
Electronic tattoos: Using distinctive body locations to control mobile devices intuitively

interactions.title: Tap your knuckles like buttons, then flatten your hand to scrub
interactions.description: A touch tattoo on the knuckles reads a fist as four discrete buttons sitting on the protruding peaks, and reads a flat hand as a smooth slider running along the same row of knuckles, so the same patch changes function with your pose.

interactions.title: A music remote on the side of your index finger
interactions.description: A bend-plus-touch display worn along the index finger acts as a volume slider when the finger is straight, and snaps into three play/pause, next, and previous buttons when you bend the finger, with the lit-up segments showing which mode is active.

interactions.title: Slide your thumb along a finger crease to change volume while running
interactions.description: The Wrinkle Slide places a touch sensor over a flexure line on a finger, so you can feel the crease through the thin tattoo and slide your thumb along it to adjust a value eyes-free, with a tap to select — built as a one-handed remote for a mobile music player.

interactions.title: Squeeze the bump on your wrist to catch a virtual creature
interactions.description: The CaptureMark, a circular tattoo over the ulna bump on the wrist, distinguishes a touch from a squeeze: when an AR game pings you about a nearby object, you squeeze the tattoo to try to catch it, and it blinks and then glows to confirm the capture.

interactions.title: Touch a heart over your birthmark to call a loved one
interactions.description: The HeartMark is a touch-sensitive heart-shaped display laid over a birthmark that lights up to signal a chosen person is available; tapping it places a call to them, using the existing skin mark as a personal, memorable button location.

interactions.title: Touch your wedding ring to make your partner's ring glow
interactions.description: A hidden tattoo under a plain wedding ring makes the ring touch-sensitive and adds a sliver of display around it, so touching your own ring sends a soft glow to your partner's ring for subtle, wordless contact — without modifying the ring itself.

interactions.title: A discreet glance-free accept/decline for incoming calls
interactions.description: Building on the demonstrated wrinkle toggle, a slim two-electrode sensor over a finger crease could let you swipe one way across the wrinkle to accept a call and the other way to decline, feeling the crease as your guide so you never look at a screen.

interactions.title: An eyes-free brightness or temperature dial worn on a knuckle
interactions.description: Because the knuckle slider already supports tactile-tick continuous input, the same patch could be mapped to a home dimmer or thermostat, letting you run a thumb across your knuckles to nudge a value up or down while your hands stay busy.
